pages.text: Theoretical and experimental analysis of timber-to-timber joints connected with inclined screws Roberto Tomasi *, Alessandro Crosatti, Maurizio Piazza University of Trento, Department of Mechanical and Structural Engineering, Via Mesiano 77, 38050 Trento, Italy article info Article history: Received 8 July 2009 Received in revised form 17 March 2010 Accepted 23 March 2010 Available online 27 April 2010 Keywords: Timber-to-timber connection Inclined screws Load-bearing capacity Slip modulus Eurocode 5 abstract The aim of this paper is to propose a method for calculating the load-bearing capacity and stiffness of screws placed in an inclined position with respect to the shear plane and subjected to shear–compression load, to shear–tension load or to a combination due to their crossed X-position. At the same time, the authors also wish to highlight how the current method indicated by Eurocode 5 is partially unsuitable for describing the experimental results of strength and stiffness for these particular conﬁgurations. To this regard, push-out tests were carried out and the analysis of the resulting load–displacement curves has led to the deﬁnition of the main parameters describing the mechanical behaviour of the connections: strength, stiffness, effective number, ductility. The experimental values of strength and stiffness were then compared with the theoretical ones obtained using the Eurocode 5 calculation method and the cal- culation method proposed in this paper. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction The resistance of wood connections, using metal fasteners with cylindrical shanks placed perpendicularly with respect to the shear plane and loaded perpendicularly to their axis can be calculated using Johansen’s yielding theory [1], depending on the embedment strength of the timber element and on the yield moment of the fas- tener. The recent availability on the market of self tapping screws with continuous threads, characterized by high withdrawal capac- ity, has led the opportunity to conceive new ‘‘engineered” geomet- rical conﬁguration in order to produce connection endowed with higher values of stiffness and load-carrying capacities with respect to the traditional conﬁguration. The screws are not placed perpen- dicularly to the interface between the members to be connected but they are arranged with various angles between the screw axis and the grain direction ( Fig. 2). Therefore the load transfer mecha- nism involves not only the bending capacity of the screw and the embedment strength of the wood, but also the withdrawal capac- ity of the fasteners as well as the friction between the timber ele- ments induced by the geometrical conﬁguration. Moreover the possibility to insert the screws without pre-drilling leads to further advantages with respect to the ‘‘traditional” conﬁguration, where higher diameter dowel type connectors are usually adopted, in terms of simplicity, ease and quickness of assembly. For timber-to-timber connections with screws inserted at an inclination a with respect to the line perpendicular to the shear plane (Fig. 1 ), new parameters are involved that affect the load- bearing capacity of the connection in comparison with the tradi- tional conﬁguration where the Johansen model can be applied: the screws withdrawal strength, the angle between the screw axis and the force, and the friction between the timber elements at the shear plane interface. Consequently, for those cases, it is extre

2.2. Proposed calculation method for calculating load-bearing capacity The theoretical study, supported by experimental results, has led to propose a calculation method adopting: (a) for screws subjected to shear–compression stress (Fig. 2 a), inequality (1) with quadratic combination of stresses and strengths proposed by Eurocode 5 [8], therefore Eq. (3); (b) for screws subjected to shear–tension stress (Fig. 2b) and perpendicular to the shear plane, the calculation model by Bejtka and Blaß presented in [2] and (c) for X-positioned screws ( Fig. 2 c), working simultaneously one under shear–tension and one under shear–compression stress, the calculation model by Bejtka and Blaß [2] adapted taking into account the fact that the resisting system con- sists of two crossed screws. The following equations, excerpted from [2], allow the calcula- tion of the characteristic load-bearing capacity parallel to the shear plane (per fastener and per shear plane) for screws subjected to shear–tension stress with respect to a single shear plane. (5), the load-bearing capacity parallel to the shear plane Fv,Rk of timber-to-timber connections with inclined screws subjected to shear–tension stress is, therefore, equal to: Fax;Rk ¼ Rax;k ¼ min Rax;1;k Rax;2;k /C26/C27 ð6Þ Fv;Rk ¼ minfRa; Rb; Rc ; Rd; Re; Rf gð 7Þ Symbols in Eqs. (5) and (6) are: /C15 s1 anchorage length of the screw into the ﬁrst wood element, measured orthogonally to the shear plane; /C15 s2 anchorage length of the screw into the second wood element, measured orthogonally to the shear plane; /C15 fh,1,k characteristic embedment strength of the ﬁrst wood element; /C15 fh,2,k characteristic embedment strength of the second wood element; /C15 b = fh,2,k/fh,1,k ratio of embedment strengths; /C15 Rax,1,k characteristic withdrawal strength of the screw from the ﬁrst wood element; /C15 Rax,2,k characteristic withdrawal strength of the screw from the second wood element; /C15 d effective diameter of the screw; /C15 Myk characteristic yield moment of the screw; /C15 l friction coefﬁcient at the interface between wood elements and /C15 Ri characteristic load-bearing capacity parallel to the shear plane, associated with the ‘‘i th” rupture, where i = a– f. In the case of X-positioned screws, the characteristic load-bear- ing capacity parallel to the shear plane (per fastener and per shear plane) is onc

3.2. Specimen geometry The samples used are those commonly employed in push-out tests, i.e. they consist of two lateral wood elements and a central one, held together by fasteners, with the geometry depicted in Fig. 6 . For standardization purposes, in the various specimens the fasteners were anchored at inclinations differing by 15 /C176incre- ments. The result was screws anchored at 0 /C176,1 5/C176,3 0/C176or 45 /C176with respect to the perpendicular to the shear plane. The layout of the screws with respect to the direction and side of the external load was chosen so as to obtain: (a) single inclined screws working only under shear–compres- sion load ( a/C176Ai nFigs. 6 and 7 ); (b) single inclined screws working only under shear–tension load ( a/C176Bi nFig. 6 ) and (c) screws crossed in X-position with the same inclination a working simultaneously under shear–compression and shear–tension stress ( a/C176Xi nFig. 7 ). In the case of connections with 0 /C176and 15/C176, screws 190 mm long were used; in the case of 30 /C176and 45 /C176, screws 220 mm long were preferred. In all cases, the screw anchorage was performed so as to position the midpoint of the smooth shank of the fastener at the slip plane level. The anchorage depth of the tip complied with the minimum value of 6 /C1d , as recommended in [10], in order for the connection to function effectively under shear stress, excluding the possibility of screw withdrawal. Throughout the test campaign, Eurocode 5 was used as refer- ence; therefore, the diameter of the smooth part of the screw being greater than 6 mm (see Table 1 ), reference was made to the rules applied to bolts (see Table 2 ). In the connection, the screws were placed so that, each one would engage only one shear plane. Specimens were fabricated with two fasteners on each side (indicated by ‘2 + 2’ in Fig. 7 ), and with four fasteners (indicated by ‘4 + 4E’ and ‘4 + 4’ in Fig. 7 ). For what concerns the spacing a 1 parallel to the grain, both the minimum value of Table 2 (a1 =7 0m m /C25 7/C1d > 62 mm; specimens identiﬁed by ‘4 + 4’) and the 18 /C1d value (160 mm per d = 8.9 mm; specimens identiﬁed by ‘4 + 4E’) were observed. This was neces- sary for obtaining ‘efﬁcient’ screws, for which the theoretical num- ber n ef, calculated by means of Eq. (20) reported in the following, would be equal to the actual number of fasteners n. In Fig. 7 plans and prospects of the specimens are shown only with inclination a =4 5/C176A and with the X layout (seeFig. 7 ). As to the other inclinations, the types are the same and, therefore, in the case of screws with single inclination a/C176A anda/C176B, types 2 + 2, 4 + 4E and 4 + 4 will result while, in the case of X-positioned screws with a/C176X, only type 2 + 2 will result. 3.3. Set-up, instruments, test protocol In push-out tests the specimens were subjected to a compres- sive stress under monotone displacement control, using a universal machine able to apply loads up to 1000 kN. The machine allows to impose displacements via a hydraulic actuator, the deformation speed of which is manually set to a constant mean value falling within the 0.02 /C4 0.2 mm/s range, in accordance with standards EN 12512 [17] and EN 26891 [11]. The maximum load may be reached in about 10 /C4 15 min. The loads acting on the specimens were measured using a load cell, placed between the jack and the specimen. Displacements were measured using four linear variable differ- ential transformer (LVDT) transduc

state standards), can be computed by means of the following expression ks ¼ 0:4 /C1 Fest /C0 0:1 /C1 Fest v0:4 /C0 v0:1 ð16Þ where /C15 v0.1, assembly slip (equivalent to load 0.1/C1F est); /C15 v0.4, assembly slip equivalent to load 0.4/C1F est; The estimated maximum load, Fest, was deﬁned as the average of the maximum load Fmax,i of various specimens of the same type, verifying that condition b) below be satisﬁed: ðaÞ Fest ¼ mean ðFmax;1; Fmax;2;...;F max;N Þ ðbÞ 0:8 /C1 Fmax;i 6 Fest 6 1:2 /C1 Fmax;i ð17Þ The maximum load of each test, Fmax, corresponds to the ﬁrst of the two following conditions occurring during a test: actual maxi- mum load (F max,R), or load corresponding to a 15 mm slip (F 15). 3.5. Analysis of force versus joint slip curves according to EN 12512 European standard EN 12512 [17] speciﬁes a test method for determining the ductility, strength decrease, and energy dissipa- tion characteristics of connections assembled with mechanical fas- teners consequent to the application of a cyclic load. The standard deﬁnes ductility as the capacity of the connection to withstand large displacements within the plastic ﬁeld without a substantial reduction in strength. Its measurement is obtained through the fol- lowing ratio: D ¼ vu vy ð18Þ where: /C15 vu is the maximum admissible slip, i.e. the slip of the joint cor- responding to the maximum admissible load Fu and /C15 vy is the yield slip, i.e. the slip of the joint corresponding to the yield load Fy. The yield load Fy is the load value at entry into the plastic ﬁeld. It, therefore, corresponds to the separation point between the elas- tic arm and the plastic arm of the force versus slip, F– v, curve. For this reason it is normally indicated as the ‘‘yielding point”. The CEN Standard [17] provides two methods for determining F y according to the shape of the F– v curve: (a) in the case of a markedly bi-linear behaviour, the two pro- gressions are approximated with two straight lines, the point of intersection of which indicates the yield limit and (b) in the case of a decidedly non-linear behaviour, the elastic segment is approximated with a straight line passing through the two points 0.1 /C1F u and 0.4 /C1F u, where Fu is the maximum admissible load, while the plastic segment is approximated with a straight line with inclination equal to 1/6 of the inclination of the ﬁrst straight line. Fig. 8. Specimen placed inside the testing machine (METROCOM) with assembled LVDT transducers (right); control panel (left). Fig. 9. Average force versus displacement, F– v, curves for various inclinations a; cases 2 + 2, 4 + 4, 4 + 4E, 2 + 2X. 1566 R. Tomasi et al. / Construction and Building Materials 24 (2010) 1560–1571

The maximum admissible load, Fu, is the load value correspond- ing to the ﬁrst of the following conditions obtained during the test: (a) failure; (b) 80% of Fmax (on the descending arm), when the maximum point is reached with a slip less than 30 mm and (c) a 30 mm slip of the joint. 3.6. Experimental results Fig. 9 reports the experimental result in force–displacement diagrams for all specimens geometries considered during the experimental campaign. Figs. 10–12 report the experimental results, in terms of maxi- mum load, effective number, stiffness and ductility, obtained by processing the 64 push-out tests. 3.7. Maximum load In Fig. 10 and Table 3 the term ‘‘maximum load” obviously indi- cates, as in Section 3.4, the maximum admissible load found according to the EN 26891 standard [11]. The experimental effective number was deﬁned by applying the following expression: nef ¼ Fmax;multiple Fmax;single ð19Þ where: /C15 Fmax,multiple is the value of Fmax relating to several fasteners in line carried out from tests identiﬁed as 4 + 4E, 4 + 4 and /C15 Fmax,single is the value of Fmax relating to a single fastener carried out from tests identiﬁed as 2 + 2. Fmax is the maximum load or the maximum admissible load de- ﬁned as described by the EN 26891 standard [11]. The theoretical effective number was found using the expression provided by Eurocode 5 and applicable to bolted connections, the diameter of the smooth part of the screw shaft being greater than 6 mm: nef ¼ min n; n0:9 /C1 ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ a1 13 /C1 d 4 r ! ð20Þ In expression (20) the greater diameter of the threaded part of the screws, d = 8.9 mm, was used. Tables 4 and 5 report the exper- imental results in term of effective number. In Fig. 11 and Table 6 the term experimental stiffness indicates the value of ks found as indicated in Section 3.4 according to the EN 26891 standard [11]. It can be useful to note that the theoretical value Kser for stiff- ness, according to expression (4) resulted 9083 kN/m in case 2 + 2 and 18,166 kN/m in cases 4 + 4E and 4 + 4. The term static ductility D s is used here, because in the tests the loads were applied at such a slow speed that they can be consid- Fig. 10. ered almost static. Static ductility is deﬁned based on the indica- tions of Section 3.5, in compliance with standard EN 12512 [17], and results are reported in Fig. 12 and Table 7 . 4. Comparison between experimental results and calculated values With reference to the methods for calculating the stiffness and the load-bearing capacity described in Chapter 2, the experimental results are here compared to the corresponding theoretical values. 4.1. Indications concerning the calculation of strength and stiffness Strength and stiffness were calculated according to both the cal- culation method indicated in Eurocode 5 and that proposed in this paper at Sections 2.1 and 2.2 , with following parameters: (a) Mean wood density qm = 426 kg/m3, characteristic wood density qk = 402 kg/m3 (as per Section 3.1); (b) Friction coefﬁcient between wood elements, in compliance with the assumptions of Eurocode 5, equal to l = 0.25; (c) Characteristic yield moment of the screw, Myk, equal to 23,300 N mm based on the indications given in the general homologation certiﬁcate for building construction issued by the German Institute for Building Technology, see [15] and (d) Screw withdrawal/penetration strength not greater than the ultimate tensile strength (22,000 N) and the value associated with the ultimate lateral instability limit state (12,400 N) speciﬁed in the general homologation certiﬁcate for building construction. As to the calculation method of Eurocode 5, for the purpose of calculating the embedment strength, expression (21) was used, with d = d ef: fh;k ¼ 0:082 /C1ð 1 /C0 0:01 /C1 dÞ/C1 qk in N =mm2 ð21Þ For the calculation method proposed here, for the embedment strength, expression (22) provided by Blaß et al. with various incli- nations of the screw axis with respect to the grain [18] was used, with d = def, derived from a research study on a large assortment of screws ( e =0 /C176screw parallel to the grain, e =9 0/C176screw perpen- dicular to the grain). fh;e;k ¼ 0:019 /C1 q1:24 k /C1 d /C0 0:3 2:5 /C1 cos2 e þ sin2e in N =mm2 ð22Þ It is easy to verify that, by setting e =9 0/C176, the embedding value provided by (22) is about equal to that established by other stan- dards for non-predrilled nails. As also speciﬁed in their homologa- tion certiﬁcate, these screws must be subjected to the rules applicable to non-pre-drilled nails and not to those applicable to bolts, as provided by Eurocode 5. When applying the calculation method proposed above, the fol- lowing three speciﬁcations should be kept in mind. (1) The ﬁrst speciﬁcation regards the calculation of the with- drawal/penetration strength Fax,Rk indicated in Eq. (6), per- formed here according to the indications given in the general homologation certiﬁcate for building construction [15]. For the characteristic withdrawal/penetration strength, within the range of inclinations considered in this study, i.e. 45/C176A6 a 6 45/C176B, a constant characteristic value that is inde- pendent of the embedment angle with respect to the grain, is provided: Fax;Rk ¼ f1;k /C1 lef /C1 d in N ð23Þ f1;k ¼ 100 /C1 10/C0 6 /C1 q2 k in N =mm2 ð24Þ where: /C15 qk is the characteristic density of the wood, in kg/m 3; /C15 d is the outer diameter of the screw thread, in mm; /C15 lef is the effective embedment length, i.e. the length of the thread in the ith wood element including the drilling tip, in mm; /C15 f1,k is the characteristic unit withdrawal/penetration strength. The value of Fax,Rk to be used in (6) i

5. Discussions on the experimental results The experimental values for the maximum load-bearing capac- ity show that inclined screws of the type examined: /C15 have approximately the same load-bearing capacity when sub- jected to shear–compression load (cases 45 /C176A, 30/C176A, 15/C176A); /C15 have a load-bearing capacity that increases with angle a, reach- ing maximum values when a =3 0/C176and 45 /C176, when subjected to shear–tension load (cases 45 /C176B, 30/C176B, 15/C176B); /C15 have a load-bearing capacity that increases with angle a, reach- ing maximum values when a =4 5/C176, placed in X-position (cases 45/C176X, 30/C176X, 15 /C176X); /C15 among all cases, the maximum value of load-bearing capacity is obtained when the screws are crossed in X-position at an angle a =4 5/C176and are subjected to shear–tension load. The observation of the experimental values in terms of slip modulus shows that the inclined screws: /C15 achieve approximately the same stiffness when subjected to shear–compression load (cases 45 /C176A, 30/C176A, 15/C176A) and perpen- dicular to the shear plane (case 0 /C176); /C15 have stiffness that grows with the angle a of the screws, reach- ing maximum values for a =3 0/C176and 45 /C176when subjected to shear–tension load (cases 45 /C176B, 30/C176B, 15/C176B); /C15 have stiffness that grows with the angle a, reaching maximum values for a =4 5/C176when crossed in X-position (cases 45 /C176X, 30/C176X,15/C176X); /C15 among all cases, the maximum value of stiffness is obtained when the screws are crossed in X-position at an angle a =4 5/C176 and are subjected to shear–tension load; these values amount to 6 /C4 8 times those at 0 /C176. The experimental tests gave for all specimens a ‘‘type f” ductile rupture mode, featuring wood embedment and the formation of two plastic hinges in the screw, as anticipated by Eurocode 5 and by the calculation method proposed. For screws subjected to shear–tension and shear–compression loads, all experimental results indicate that, independently from the inclination and from the distance a 1 adopted, no reduction of strength has been observed due to the presence of several screws aligned parallel to the grain. This means that, for the cases studied, n = nef, where n is the number of screws along each line, and nef is the effective number of screws in the same line: this is true there- fore both for case 4 + 4E where a 1 =1 8/C1d , and for case 4 + 4 where a1 =7 /C1d . The expression proposed by Eurocode 5, here in Eq. (20),i s therefore completely unsuitable to describe the experimental data, particularly when the number of screws along each line is n =2 . Testing has pointed out a static ductility decrease with the in- crease of angle a (Fig. 12). The static ductility reaches its maximum value Ds /C25 20 /C4 30 when the fasteners are perpendicular to the shear plane, resulting always greater than four for all values of a and stress conditions of the screws. 6. Conclusions For the evaluation of the load-bearing capacity, the following comments can be summed up: /C15 Eurocode 5 calculation method is mostly unfavourable in terms of safety in the case of screws subjected to shear–compression load ( g < 1). It is worth noting that in this method the embed- ding strength is expressed by Eq. (21) which does not take into account the inclination of the screw axis with respect to the grain. On the contrary, it is favourable in terms of safety in the case of screws subjected to shear–tens

the load-bearing capacity according to Johansen, expressed by the ﬁrst term of Eq. (8.6) (refer to what reported in point 8.7.2 (2) of Eurocode) and (c) the term Fax,Rk/4 can be written also as l Fax,Rk, where l = 0.25 is the friction coefﬁcient for wood to wood surfaces. This term must be taken into account for screws subjected to shear–tension stress, while it must be disregarded for screws subjected to shear–compression stress (under the latter stress condition the wood elements tend to separate and, therefore, friction forces cannot develop). The characteristic load-bearing capacity parallel to the shear plane F v,Rk can be written in terms of the characteristic values for axial and lateral terms according to Fig. 1 : Fax;Ek ¼ Fv;Rk /C1 sin a; Fl;Ek ¼ Fv;Rk /C1 cos a ð2Þ Then, writing Eq. (1) in terms of characteristic values and adopt- ing the expressions (2) for axial strength Fax,Rk and lateral strength Fl,Rk, the characteristic load-bearing capacity parallel to the shear plane Fv,Rk can be written as: Fv;Rk ¼ 1 sen a Fax;Rk /C16/C17 2 þ cos a Fl;Rk /C16/C17 2/C20/C21 1=2 ð3Þ Since the type of connection under investigation is not explicitly contemplated by Eurocode 5, the stiffness, or instantaneous slip modulus, K ser of joints with inclined screws is determined with the same formulas used for screws inserted perpendicularly to the shear plane and, consequently, by means of the expression re- ported in Table 7.1 of EN 1995-1-1: Kser ¼ q1:5 m /C1 d=23 ð4Þ The values of Kser indicated in the following paragraphs are de- ﬁned using: (a) the value of qm determined experimentally during the test campaign and (b) the effective screw diameter def = 1.1 dcore, where dcore is the thread root diameter.
